title: Weeping Time Documentary: The Beginning of an IVF Conversation
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman uses the personal IVF tragedy of an anonymous Christian couple, paired with C.R. Cali's new Weeping Time documentary, to argue that IVF is a largely ignored ethical disaster in which embryonic image bearers are routinely killed, frozen, or discarded. The piece frames IVF participation as idolatry rooted in self-reliance rather than trust in God, and calls the Church to engage the practice with the seriousness it gives abortion.
audiences: Pro-life Christian who has not considered IVF ethics
Skeptic questioning the moral status of embryos

questions.question: How does Mr. P. describe the underlying motivation that led him and his wife to choose IVF despite his prior moral objections?
questions.answer: He frames it as a self-reliant attempt to complete what felt like a missing piece of their life, an effort to seize their own happiness rather than trust God with their childlessness.
questions.quote: It was a very morally compromised decision to go forward with IVF, and the reason why we did is because we felt like we were missing something in our life. You look at it sort of like a puzzle, right? There was a piece of our puzzle that was empty, and if we don't have a child, our puzzle will never be complete.

questions.question: What happened to the seven embryos the P. family created through IVF?
questions.audience: Skeptic questioning the moral status of embryos
questions.answer: All seven died, and the couple was informed in a cold meeting at the fertility office four days after the procedure.
questions.quote: They received a call four days later asking them to come to the fertility office for a meeting in which they were coldly told that *all* of their embryos had died.

questions.question: How does Mr. P. distinguish his view of his lost embryos from how he believes many other Christians regard them?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who has not considered IVF ethics
questions.answer: He explicitly counts the embryos as his children whose deaths he grieved, contrasting himself with Christians who treat the embryo as something less.
questions.quote: I don't have the problem that a lot of Christians do today where they look at the embryo as something other than their child. So for me, it was very emotional. It was basically — my children died,

questions.question: What does the Weeping Time documentary cover, and who is featured in it?
questions.answer: It is a C.R. Cali production that interviews IVF families, embryo-adopting families, an IVF doctor, and theologians including Dusty Deevers, Blake Gideon, Kelly Greene, Owen Strachan, and George Grant.
questions.quote: The project features interviews with families who have participated in traditional IVF, families who have adopted abandoned embryos, an IVF doctor, and theologians including Dusty Deevers, Blake Gideon, Kelly Greene, Owen Strachan, and George Grant.

questions.question: How did the broader Christian ethics academy respond when Cali approached them about the IVF documentary?
questions.answer: Silberman notes that none of the numerous Christian ethics professors Cali contacted were willing to be interviewed on the subject.
questions.quote: (While a number of pastors and theologians are featured, not one of the numerous Christian ethics professors Cali approached for an interview wanted anything to do with this subject matter.)

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue abolitionists must not let attention to abortion eclipse the IVF issue?
questions.answer: Because the IVF industry is also waging a war on the image of God in human life, even while the Church is rightly turning more attention to abortion.
questions.quote: Abortion is rightly beginning to receive the level of attention from the Church it has long deserved, but we must not overlook the war against the image of God being waged by the IVF industry and its proponents.

questions.question: According to Dr. John David Gordon, why does standard IVF practice produce so many surplus embryos?
questions.audience: Skeptic questioning the moral status of embryos
questions.answer: Working with a single egg is inefficient, so doctors use fertility drugs to bring multiple eggs to fertilization, which inevitably leaves more embryos than parents intend to implant.
questions.quote: When IVF started, we were just working with the one egg…The problem is going after one egg isn't as efficient because you might not get that egg, the egg may not fertilize, it might not grow. So using fertility drugs, what a doctor is doing is rescuing those extra eggs…so rather than just one egg getting to the finish line, multiple eggs are getting to the finish line…The problem is, as soon as you have more embryos than you're comfortable placing back in utero, now you have to do something with those extra embryos…

questions.question: What estimate does Dr. Gordon give for the number of embryos currently frozen in the United States, and how fast is that number growing?
questions.answer: He estimates at least 1.5 million frozen embryos nationally, growing by roughly 18 percent each year, many of whom were never reclaimed by their parents.
questions.quote: at least probably 1.5 million frozen embryos in the country and it goes up by about 18 percent a year.

questions.question: How does Dusty Deevers define idolatry in the context of the IVF discussion?
questions.answer: He defines it as elevating something good into an ultimate thing whose absence makes you unable to be satisfied.
questions.quote: Idolatry is taking a good thing and make it an ultimate thing, such that you cannot be satisfied unless you have it.

questions.question: How does C.R. Cali extend Deevers' definition of idolatry as it applies to fertility treatment?
questions.answer: Cali adds that idolatry pushes a person to do anything to obtain the idolized thing because it has become the source of their happiness and identity.
questions.quote: And you would be willing to do anything to get that thing because that's the source of your happiness. That's the source of your identity.

questions.question: How does Mr. P. summarize his repentance and the moral logic he had used to justify IVF?
questions.answer: He calls his participation an evil means to a good end, an idolatrous self-deception in which his envisioned life justified the unethical method.
questions.quote: I have repented of my involvement in IVF. It is something I deeply regret. As I wrap it up from a conclusionary standpoint, I was willing to participate in an unethical and evil way of trying to accomplish a good thing. But the ends don't justify the means. I think so many couples get to the place where they self-deceive; at least I did, where it was the ends will justify the means. At the end of the day, it's idolatry, but it's basically — what I've envisioned for my life, that coming to fruition justifies it.

questions.question: What lesson did Mr. P. draw from Job that reframed how he and his wife approached infertility?
questions.answer: He concluded that, like Job, infertile couples are ultimately left with the question of whether they will trust God, even without an explanation, and that trust must be enough.
questions.quote: I guess after that reflection and prayer, I came to the realization that that is basically what we are left with when it comes to infertility. We are left with the decision — and I do believe God gives us the decision — 'are you going to trust me?' And at the end of the day, that has to be enough.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the value of Cali's work for the Church given his limited resources?
questions.answer: He calls the documentary an invaluable resource produced on a small budget that pushes the Church to think more deeply about IVF's theology and impact on image bearers.
questions.quote: Cali, a small town pastor, did not have a large budget when he began this project, but he has provided an invaluable resource to the Church that we may come to think more deeply about this practice, its theological implications, and its impact upon God's image bearers.

related.title: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
related.reason: Develops the theological premise underlying this article's claim that IVF wages war on God's image bearers.

related.title: More Wack Analogies and Scripture-less Arguments From Scott Klusendorf
related.reason: Another Silberman piece tagged with IVF that critiques pro-life evasiveness on embryonic personhood.

related.title: C.R. Cali: How to Exhort Legislators Who Support Abolition and Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Profiles C.R. Cali, the pastor who produced the Weeping Time documentary discussed here.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Parallels the article's idolatry framing, applied to the pro-life movement's institutional posture.

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Connects the embryonic-personhood logic of this article to legislative fights over protection beginning at conception.

related.title: Immediate Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Provides the abolitionist ethical frame Silberman implicitly applies when condemning IVF as ends-justify-means reasoning.
